steps.title: Compare the two data sets
steps.content: Set A is $\{8,11,12,15,21,27\}$. The first five values of Set B are $\{9,12,13,16,22\}$, which are exactly the first five values of A each increased by $1$. So whether $\text{SD}(B)$ is smaller or larger than $\text{SD}(A)$ depends entirely on how $h$ compares to $27+1=28$.

steps.title: Key fact about adding a constant
steps.content: Adding the same constant to every value in a data set shifts the mean by that constant but leaves the standard deviation unchanged. So if $h=28$, Set B is just Set A shifted by $+1$, giving $\text{SD}(B)=\text{SD}(A)$. To make $\text{SD}(A)>\text{SD}(B)$, $h$ must be closer to the rest of the data than $28$ is — that is, between roughly the mean of the other five values and $28$.

steps.title: Test each option
steps.content:
A quick calculator check is to enter each possible data set as a list and compare population standard deviations:
$$\text{SD}(A)\approx6.75$$
$$\text{SD}(B_{23})\approx4.79$$
$$\text{SD}(B_{28})\approx6.75$$
$$\text{SD}(B_{29})\approx7.45$$ Only I, $h=23$, makes Set B less spread out than Set A.

steps.title: Match to a choice
steps.content:
• A) I only — matches our result.
  • B) II only — II gives equal SDs, not greater. Eliminated.
  • C) III only — III gives SD(B)$>$SD(A). Eliminated.
  • D) II or III — neither works. Eliminated.
That matches choice A — A is correct.
